title: Connecting Noodles

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: You have 100 cooked noodles, giving 200 free ends. Blindfolded, you repeatedly pick TWO ENDS uniformly at random and tie them together. Continue until no free ends remain.
rules: Each tie connects two random ends -- could be ends of the same noodle (creating a closed loop) or different noodles (merging them into one longer noodle).
Stop when all ends are tied.
We want E[number of closed loops formed].
setup.question: What is the expected number of closed loops?

learningObjectives: Set up a recurrence on E[f(n)] = expected loops with n noodles.
P(loop forms at this step | n noodles) = 1/(2n - 1) (one specific end completes a loop out of 2n - 1 candidates).

stages.label: Set up the step probability
stages.prompt: Start with n noodles (2n free ends). Pick one end at random. The other end you tie it to is chosen from the 2n - 1 remaining ends. What is P(this tie creates a loop)?

options.text: 1/(2n - 1).  Of the 2n - 1 remaining ends, exactly ONE belongs to the same noodle as the first end -- tying them creates a loop.

stages.explanation: Only one of the 2n - 1 candidates is the OTHER end of the same noodle. Probability = 1/(2n - 1).

stages.label: Set up the recurrence
stages.prompt: If the tie creates a loop, you've added 1 loop and reduced to n - 1 noodles (the looped noodle is gone). If not, you've merged two noodles into one, leaving n - 1 noodles (no new loop). What's the recurrence for E[f(n)]?

options.text: E[f(n)] = E[f(n-1)] + 1/(2n-1).  Both branches end with n - 1 noodles, but only the first adds a loop.

stages.explanation: Either way you proceed with n - 1 noodles. The expected new loops at this step is 1 * 1/(2n - 1) + 0 * (2n - 2)/(2n - 1) = 1/(2n - 1).

stages.label: Solve the recurrence
stages.prompt: Iterate down to E[f(1)] = 1 (one noodle, two ends -- tying them always creates a loop). E[f(n)] = ?

stages.label: Commit for n = 100
stages.prompt: E[f(100)] = 1 + 1/3 + 1/5 + ... + 1/199. Compute to 4 decimals. (Hint: this sum approaches (1/2)*ln(2*100) + (gamma + ln 2)/2 ~ 3.28.)
stages.explanation: Sum of odd reciprocals 1 + 1/3 + ... + 1/199 ≈ 3.28. So expected ≈ 3 loops in the noodle problem -- surprising for 100 noodles.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Compare to the harmonic series H_100 = 1 + 1/2 + ... + 1/100 ≈ 5.19. Why are 'odd reciprocals' a different sum?
Show that the sum is approximately (1/2)*ln(2n) for large n.
What's E[f(1)]? E[f(2)]? Verify by direct enumeration.
